findings.topic: PPV looping / post-purchase momentum
findings.title: PPV Looping: Follow up within 5–10 seconds of any purchase to chain sales
findings.claim: The best time to sell a subscriber another PPV is immediately after they make any purchase, capitalizing on the 'f*** it' impulse to keep the buying momentum going.
findings.what_they_do: Chatters monitor their CRM (e.g. Infloww) for any purchase notification — old PPV, mass PPV, feed post unlock — and follow up within 5–10 seconds with a curiosity-hook message before the fan even finishes watching what they bought. The follow-up doesn't reference the old video; it teases the next thing ('I was literally just about to do something I don't usually do'). From that reply, the chatter plans to sell 5 sequential PPVs, each progressively more explicit or longer, so each one appears more valuable and justifies a higher price. This loop restarts every time the fan buys.
findings.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is fully outsourced (§8). There is no internal SOP, chatter workflow, or CRM trigger process documented. This is a concrete, mechanics-level system that would form the core of Palm's future in-house chat team playbook.
findings.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team, make PPV looping the foundational selling framework. Define the CRM trigger (purchase notification), set a hard response-time SLA of <30 seconds, write curiosity-hook templates for the follow-up message, and require chatters to plan a 5-PPV ladder before entering any loop. Build this into chatter onboarding SOPs.

sources.quote: the best time to sell something to a subscriber is right after they've made a purchase
chatters know the second they get that notification on their inflow or whatever CRM you use, that they need to follow up with the subscriber instantly

findings.topic: Fan segmentation — low vs high spenders
findings.title: Segment fans by max spend ($50 vs $120+) and use different opening tactics per tier
findings.claim: Low/non-spenders need fast, transactional, no-brainer openings, while high spenders need slower, personalized, emotionally invested approaches that reflect the higher ROI of putting time into them.
findings.what_they_do: Two distinct opening playbooks: (1) Low/non-spenders (<$50 max purchase): 'Two choices' illusion (free thing + heavily discounted PPV framed as a favor); 'Question game' (ask 2–3 preference questions about color/toy/angle, disappear briefly, return with a PPV 'chosen based on their answers'). Goal is just to get the first purchase and enter the loop. (2) High spenders (>$120 max purchase): 'Opinion opening' (frame a PPV as something the creator is nervous/unsure about, ask for their trusted opinion — if they object to price, say you're 'impartial' and it makes them more curious); 'Personalized PPV' for lapsed high spenders (manually send a high-quality video with a caption referencing their past purchases, their interests from fan notes, priced $150–200, with fan's name in the first 5 words of the last message sent).
findings.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale-hunting module (§9) that tracks spend data, but no documented segmented selling playbook or per-tier opener scripts. The data infrastructure exists; the chatter execution layer does not.
findings.recommendation: Map these two tiers onto Palm's existing Fan Tracker/whale-hunting data. For the in-house chat team, write two distinct opener script banks keyed to max-spend threshold. The 'question game' opener is particularly low-friction and could be A/B tested quickly once chatting is brought in-house. The lapsed-whale personalized PPV ($150–200, notes-referenced caption) is a high-ROI re-engagement tactic worth standardizing.

sources.quote: if we were talking to, say, a low or non-spender, we want to be able to transition in a way that is fast and efficient and make that first pay-per-view seem like a no-brainer
it's pretty much exclusively for non-active high spenders. So people that used to spend a lot of money, but don't really talk anymore

findings.topic: PPV anchoring — pre-seeding sale context
findings.title: PPV Anchors: Plant a story or fantasy 6–24 hours before sending the PPV to manufacture perceived relevance
findings.claim: Setting up a PPV in advance — by mentioning a dream, fantasy, new outfit, or story — gives chatters a credible, personalized reason for sending the video later, making it seem far more valuable than a cold send.
findings.what_they_do: Chatter plants an anchor in DMs (e.g. 'I just had the craziest dream about doing XYZ with you in my green lingerie') or on the feed (e.g. 'just got this new outfit, what do you think?' with a photo). They do NOT sell immediately. They return 6, 12, or 24 hours later referencing the anchor ('I couldn't help but make that dream a reality — I put on that green lingerie and…') and send the PPV. The delay is what makes it feel authentic rather than scripted. Feed-level anchors (outfit post, emotional story post) can hit many subs simultaneously, with chatters following up individually in DMs.
findings.vs_us: No internal SOP for pre-seeding PPV context exists (§8 outsourced, §2 thin on PPV vault strategy). Palm does have a content posting pipeline (§1) and scheduling infrastructure (§5), which could be used to coordinate feed-level anchors with chatter follow-up timing.
findings.recommendation: Build a 'PPV anchor' protocol into the future in-house chat SOP: define anchor types (DM-personal vs feed-broadcast), specify the delay window (6–24 hrs), and create a coordination mechanism between the posting schedule (Publer/Telegram) and the chat team so chatters know when an anchor post goes live and can follow up. This is a high-leverage tactic because it uses existing content infrastructure.

sources.quote: we can set up pay-per-views in advanced in the DMs or on the main feed... this should act as lore for the pay-per-view, which gives you a reason for sending it later
that delay between when you bring up the anchor and when you go into sell it is what makes it seem authentic

findings.topic: Indirect sexting as PPV pitch mechanism
findings.title: Pitch PPVs via 'indirect sexting' — escalate a fantasy tangent, then link the PPV as the physical manifestation
findings.claim: Rather than describing the video's explicit content directly (which sounds repetitive and generic), chatters sext about an unrelated fantasy the creator 'has been having,' escalate it fully, then link the PPV as what the creator made while thinking about that fantasy.
findings.what_they_do: Chatter chooses a fantasy (threesome, free use, femdom, cheating, etc.) — ideally one the subscriber has shown interest in — and opens with 'there's something I've been thinking about but I'm embarrassed to say.' They build out the fantasy in full descriptive detail, getting the sub emotionally invested. When the sub is clearly engaged, the chatter pitches the PPV: 'I made this video thinking about doing that for the first time.' They connect specific moments in the video to the sexting ('at 2:41 I was thinking about what you said about…'). Fantasies are ranked from least to most explicit; each PPV in the loop uses the next fantasy up the ladder. This gives chatters an inexhaustible, varied pitch mechanism that doesn't repeat.
findings.vs_us: No internal chatting scripts or sexting frameworks exist at Palm (§8 outsourced). This is a build-toward tactic for the in-house chat team and AI chatting project. The AI chatting project in particular would need this fantasy-ladder logic codified as a sequencing rule.
findings.recommendation: For the in-house chat roadmap, build a 'fantasy library' per creator (ranked low-to-high explicitness) that chatters and eventually AI chatters draw from sequentially per subscriber. Document the indirect sexting structure as a script template: embarrassed open → full fantasy build → PPV link with timestamped personal connection. This is especially relevant for the AI chatting project where consistent, structured dialogue trees are required.

sources.quote: an alternative to that is sexting as if you were opening up about a fantasy that the creator has been having
you keep talking about the fantasy until they're clearly into it, and then you link that to the next pay-per-view saying something like, I made this video thinking about doing that for the first time

findings.topic: PPV price escalation ladders by fan tier
findings.title: Tiered PPV price escalation: 50% steps for low spenders, 70% steps for high spenders; break $200 cap via tip + multi-unlock
findings.claim: PPV prices should escalate by a set percentage each step within a loop, calibrated to the fan's spending tier, and the $200 OnlyFans cap is not a real ceiling — it can be bypassed via tips or multi-PPV unlocks.
findings.what_they_do: Low spenders (max purchase <$50): increase price ~50% per step. Example ladder: $35 → $55 → $85 → $125. High spenders (max purchase >$120): increase ~70% per step. Example ladder: $50 → $85 → $150 → $255. To exceed the $200 OF cap: (a) request a tip first, then send the PPV, or (b) split into multiple PPVs (e.g. 4×$200 = $800 total) framed as 'unlock these now and I'll send the main video while I get it from my archive.' Always frame multi-unlocks as a favor/bonus. If a subscriber accepts a dropped price too easily, immediately add more PPVs to the bundle to recover revenue ('I had an idea — because it's you, I want to throw in some extra stuff').
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model or escalation framework (§7 GAP — explicitly called out as the #1 creator concern). This is directly applicable intelligence for building Palm's pricing playbook.
findings.recommendation: Document these two ladders (50% / 70% escalation by tier) as Palm's default PPV pricing framework. Add the $200-bypass mechanics (tip-first, multi-unlock bundle) to chatter SOPs. Critically, also adopt the 'add more content rather than discount' rule for objection handling — this protects price integrity over time and should be a standing chatter policy.

sources.quote: for low spenders, which I would define as someone with a maximum purchase of $50, we can increase the price each time by about 50%
for higher spenders, subscribers with a maximum purchase of over $120, we can try and increase the price by about 70% each time
you can't send a pay-per-view over $200. Sure, you can't, but you can still get around that in other ways

findings.topic: Objection handling — add value before discounting
findings.title: When a subscriber objects to price, add more content first; only discount by removing content, not by lowering the price
findings.claim: Reflexively discounting when a subscriber says 'too expensive' trains them to always haggle; instead, offer more content at the same price first, and if a discount is unavoidable, remove content from the bundle rather than simply lowering the stated price.
findings.what_they_do: Rule 1 — Value-add first: if a sub resists price, add a small extra (bonus clip, picture set) and make it a huge deal, framing it as a personal favor. This gets full price paid and doesn't establish a discount expectation. Rule 2 — Renegotiation discount: if discounting is unavoidable, pre-load the PPV with a few extra pictures/clips by default. When the sub asks for less, remove those extras and drop the price — so the price reduction is tied to a concrete content reduction, not arbitrary. This preserves the perception that prices are meaningful. Rule 3 — Underpriced sub recovery: if a sub accepts a dropped price too readily, immediately propose a larger bundle ('I had an idea — I want to throw in extra stuff') to recapture upside.
findings.vs_us: No internal objection-handling framework exists at Palm (§8 outsourced). This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team and the AI chatting project, where objection-handling logic needs to be systematized.
findings.recommendation: Make 'add value before discounting, renegotiate via content removal' a non-negotiable chatter policy in the future in-house SOP. For the AI chatting project, encode this as a decision tree: objection detected → attempt value-add response → if second objection → remove content and lower price. Also document the underpriced-sub recovery move as a standard upsell trigger.

sources.quote: it is a better first response to try and give them something small to sweeten the deal, and you get paid the full amount as opposed to just discounting
if they ask for a discount, you can take that stuff out and then drop the price, so you've now renegotiated

findings.topic: PPV caption copywriting — curiosity triggers
findings.title: Use specific timestamped curiosity hooks in PPV captions to maximize unlock rate
findings.claim: The PPV caption is the last decision point before purchase; embedding specific, timestamped curiosity hooks (rather than generic hype) dramatically increases unlock rates by making the sub feel something personalized and unmissable is inside.
findings.what_they_do: Chatters/copywriters craft captions ending with one or more of these proven curiosity hooks: (1) Timestamped surprise — 'I can't believe I did that at 2:41' (specificity signals authenticity); (2) Personalized connection — 'I want to see if you can spot which part I was thinking about you'; (3) Challenge/gamification — 'There's no way you'll last past what I did at minute 1:21'; (4) Embarrassment/vulnerability — 'I'm pretty embarrassed I did that towards the end, but oh well'; (5) Physical detail — 'I'm still sore from that last part'; (6) Multi-content choice — 'Which one of those positions was your favorite?' These are used as the final sentence only; the rest of the caption continues the sexting/emotional build-up from the conversation. Specificity (exact timestamps, specific details) is emphasised as what makes hooks feel genuine vs scripted.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no PPV caption copywriting framework (§2 thin, §7 GAP on PPV). Palm does have a strong content ideation and social copywriting pipeline (§1), but that is social/top-of-funnel. This is OF vault/PPV caption writing — a distinct skill set with no current internal baseline.
findings.recommendation: Build a PPV caption template library using these five hook types for the future chat team and AI chatting project. Since Palm already has a copywriting/content analysis layer in its social pipeline (§1), consider extending that infrastructure to also produce PPV caption templates per creator, keyed to their content vault. This is a relatively quick win for the AI chatting project.

sources.quote: I wonder if you'll see the surprise I threw in there for you
I'm still sore from that last part
when you're specific, it seems genuine

findings.topic: Revenue waterfall / profit layers
findings.title: Track 5 profit layers, not just creator gross revenue
findings.claim: Creator gross revenue is the least useful number for judging agency health; founders must track five sequential layers down to owner profit per hour.
findings.what_they_do: Dylan defines a 5-level waterfall: (1) creator gross, (2) agency net after split, (3) gross profit after fulfillment costs, (4) owner profit after everything, (5) owner profit per hour. He insists the only number that matters is what the founder actually keeps, and that 'revenue is vanity, profit is sanity, cash is reality.' He frames agencies claiming $100k/month as often netting $5-30k for the founder after chatters, managers, editors, VAs, software, promo, and mistakes are paid.
findings.vs_us: Palm tracks creator gross via Inflow/FansMetric dashboards and computes agency net for invoicing (§7), but there is no documented gross-profit-per-creator, owner-profit, or owner-profit-per-hour metric. The invoicing system auto-computes splits and weekly PDFs, but stops at agency net — levels 3-5 of Dylan's waterfall are a GAP (§7).
findings.recommendation: Build a 'profit waterfall' view in the admin portal: creator gross → agency net (already computed) → gross profit (subtract per-creator fulfillment costs: chat team allocation, editor time, VA time, software) → owner profit (subtract fixed overhead) → owner profit per founder hour. Surface this weekly alongside the existing invoice dashboard. This is a high-priority instrumentation task given Palm's current THIN baseline on profitability metrics.

sources.quote: level two is the agency net revenue. So that is what you keep after the creator split
level four is owner profit. So this is what you take home after everything. This is the number that actually counts
level five is ultimately the one I would call the good metric. The owner profit per hour

findings.topic: Creator profitability scorecard
findings.title: Score every creator on gross profit, team hours, and operational drag
findings.claim: A creator profitability scorecard — covering direct costs, team hours, founder attention, growth potential, and content consistency — is the core tool that separates good business decisions from emotional guesses.
findings.what_they_do: For each creator Dylan tracks: gross revenue, agency net, direct costs (chat team, editor/VA, software, promo), gross profit, team hours required, founder hours required, growth potential, churn risk, content consistency, and operational drag. He illustrates that a creator doing $12k gross with $1k costs and zero management friction (producing $5k profit) can be more valuable than a $20k creator costing $4k in fulfillment (producing $6k profit) but consuming disproportionate attention. The scorecard feeds an A/B/C creator classification used to allocate resources and make cut decisions.
findings.vs_us: Palm has whale-hunting and Fan Tracker CRM on the fan side (§9), and invoicing computes agency net per creator (§7), but there is no per-creator profitability scorecard that includes direct fulfillment costs and operational drag. This is an explicit GAP (§7). The Airtable `Creators` table exists but is not instrumented for margin tracking.
findings.recommendation: Add a 'Creator Profitability' view to the admin portal (or Airtable `Creators` table) with fields: gross revenue, agency net, estimated fulfillment cost (chat allocation + editor hours + VA hours + tools), gross profit, estimated team hours/week, estimated founder touch/week, ABC rating, and a 'would sign again?' flag. Run the audit monthly. This directly addresses Palm's §7 GAP and feeds the retention/churn work (§9).

sources.quote: this is why you need a creator profitability scorecard. For every creator, the score is gross revenue, agency net, direct costs, gross profit, team hours required, founder involvement required, growth potential, churn risk, content consistency, and operational drag

Creator B does 12K gross. You keep 6K, but she's easy to manage. She sends content like a clockwork. She barely touches your attention. And the team cost against her is only 1K. So she produces 5K in profit.

findings.topic: Creator roster classification and culling
findings.title: Classify every creator A/B/C and allocate resources accordingly — cut C creators
findings.claim: Most agencies carry too many low-quality ('C') creators out of fear of losing gross revenue, which silently caps growth by stealing attention from better creators.
findings.what_they_do: Dylan classifies creators into three tiers: A = high upside, consistent content, coachable, scalable brand; B = potential, needs structure, worth developing; C = inconsistent, ignores feedback, creates chaos, low upside, drains team. A creators get premium attention and resources. B creators get active development. C creators get fixed, downgraded, or removed. The diagnostic question is: 'Knowing what I know now, would I sign this creator again today?' If no, there must be a reason she is still consuming company resources. He notes a 30k/month agency with 2 great creators beats a 100k/month agency with 14 nightmares.
findings.vs_us: Palm has a `Creators` table with status tracking and onboarding workflow (§6), but no documented creator-tier classification, no systematic review cadence, and no written policy on when to downgrade or drop a creator. This is a GAP (§9 retention, §7 pricing/deal structure). Palm's creator acquisition is currently reactive/Josh-DM-led (§6) with no quality filter documented.
findings.recommendation: Implement a quarterly creator audit in the admin portal: list all active creators, assign A/B/C rating based on gross profit, content compliance rate, team hours consumed, and a 'would sign again?' flag. Set explicit policies: A creators get priority chat team allocation and content pipeline slots; B creators get a 60-day improvement plan; C creators get a downgrade notice or off-boarding. Pair with the profitability scorecard (finding above) to make ratings data-driven rather than subjective.

sources.quote: the creator audit is where you classify your roster into A, B, and C
knowing what I know now, would I sign this creator again today? That is the last question. That's very important.
most agencies carry way too many Creators, and they keep them out of the fear of losing their revenue number

findings.topic: Outcome-based roles and number ownership
findings.title: Every team member must own a single measurable number — vague roles produce vague performance
findings.claim: Any metric that no one on the team explicitly owns will default back to the founder, making him the bottleneck and preventing scale.
findings.what_they_do: Dylan assigns a specific owned number to every role: setter owns qualified booked calls; closer owns cash collected; chatter manager owns chat revenue and quality; account manager owns creator retention and content compliance; creative director owns post volume and content performance; ops manager owns scorecard completion and weekly execution rhythm. He uses a 5-level accountability ladder: (1) wait to be told, (2) ask what to do, (3) recommend and act, (4) act then inform, (5) full ownership / exceptions only. Chatter managers should be at level 3-4; ops/sales managers at level 5. The coaching method to move someone from level 2→3: 'Before you come to me with a problem, bring me the problem, three options, and your recommendation.'
findings.vs_us: Palm has defined Clerk roles (super_admin, admin, editor, ai_editor, chat_manager, creator) but these are access-control roles, not outcome-ownership definitions (§10 GAP). There are no documented KPIs, scorecards, or accountability levels per role. The chat team is outsourced (§8), so the chatter manager role isn't yet internal, but the account manager, editor, and Amin (manual posting) roles have no documented owned numbers.
findings.recommendation: For each internal Palm role, define one owned number and the accountability level it should operate at: editor → content posted on schedule + revision rate (level 3-4); Amin/posting contractor → on-time post compliance rate (level 3); future chat manager → PPV conversion rate + response time (level 4-5). Add these to a simple weekly scorecard reviewed by Josh/Evan. This is foundational infrastructure before Palm scales headcount further (§10 GAP).

sources.quote: Every person on your team should own a number because any number nobody owns defaults back to you
Level three, recommend act. These people, they come to you and say, here's the problem. Here's the options. Here's my recommendation. Do you agree?
the setter owns qualified calls, the closer owns cash collected, the chatter manager owns chat revenue and quality

findings.topic: Minimum viable operating system / weekly scorecard
findings.title: Run the business on a 4-part minimum viable operating system anchored by a weekly scorecard
findings.claim: A simple four-part operating system — weekly scorecard, creator profitability tracker, outcome-based roles, and founder replacement ladder — is sufficient to escape the $100k/month trap without building a bloated corporate structure.
findings.what_they_do: Dylan's weekly scorecard covers four domains: (1) Revenue: creator gross, agency net, gross profit, owner profit, revenue per creator, top/declining performers, content compliance; (2) Acquisition: outreach sent, replies, qualified leads, booked calls, shows, closes, new creators signed; (3) Delivery: content posted, PPV performance, rebuilds, expired fans, chat performance, creator complaints; (4) Team: who hit targets, who missed, which bottleneck repeated, what was fixed. The delivery operating system specifically needs 7 pieces: weekly creator review, content output tracker, chat performance tracker, revenue breakdown, issues/bottleneck list, next week's action plan, and a clear owner for each action. The founder replacement ladder moves any recurring founder task through: do it → document it → delegate it → review the output → metrics manage it → someone else fully owns it.
findings.vs_us: Palm has strong content pipeline instrumentation (§1) and invoicing automation (§7), but no weekly business-level scorecard covering all four domains, no delivery operating system for creator accounts, and no documented founder replacement ladder (§10 GAP, §9 GAP). The SMM hub redesign (§11) addresses social content visibility but not the business-health scorecard layer Dylan describes.
findings.recommendation: Build a lightweight 'Agency Weekly Scorecard' page in the admin portal (or a recurring Airtable view) covering all four scorecard domains. Prioritize delivery tracking first (content compliance + chat performance + revenue per creator) since that's where Palm currently has the most instrumentation gaps. The founder replacement ladder should be applied immediately to Evan and Josh's recurring tasks — document and delegate at least one recurring task per week. This is a direct complement to Palm's in-flight SMM hub consolidation (§11).

sources.quote: you literally just need a very simple minimum viable operating system with four parts. A weekly scorecard, a creator profitability tracker, outcome-based roles, and the founder replacement ladder

A simple delivery operating system needs seven pieces. A weekly creator review, a content output tracker, a chat performance tracker, a revenue breakdown, an issues and bottleneck list, and next week's action plan, and a clear owner for each action.
you can't scale what you only feel. You scale what you can see every week.

findings.topic: Quality-first creator sourcing and red-flag filtering
findings.title: Identify shared traits of best creators and make acquisition deliberately pickier
findings.claim: Fulfillment problems start at acquisition — a creator who won't send content or holds unrealistic expectations was a selection failure, not a delivery failure.
findings.what_they_do: Dylan runs an acquisition audit asking: where do the best creators come from (outbound, inbound, referrals, IG, switching agencies, existing creator networks)? What traits do the best creators share (existing social proof, consistent posting history, strong personality, existing audience, responsiveness, coachability, good on camera)? Which red flags are being ignored at signing? He distinguishes acquisition as a quality game vs. volume game depending on deal structure: pure volume only makes sense if selling a low-ticket course ($497) at scale with upsells; for a full-service agency the quality game dominates. He argues most agencies sign out of desperation to grow the inflow number and then complain about fulfillment 30 days later.
findings.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is currently Josh-DM-led with no documented qualification criteria, scoring rubric, or red-flag checklist (§6 GAP). The onboarding form wizard captures name, email, commission %, state, contract — but nothing about creator quality signals. This is one of Palm's biggest identified gaps (§6).
findings.recommendation: Create a creator qualification scorecard used before any call is booked: existing monthly OF revenue, social following size and growth rate, content posting consistency (check IG grid), responsiveness in initial DMs, niche fit, willingness to follow direction (ask directly), and whether they've had a previous agency (and why they left). Score each prospect. Set a minimum threshold to proceed to a close call. Add a 'red flags observed' field to the `Creators` Airtable record. This directly addresses §6 GAP and will reduce C-creator drag identified in the retention finding above.

sources.quote: most agencies are nowhere near picky enough. They're so desperate to sign creators that they just ignore any of the red flags and then complain about fulfillment 30 days later
fulfillment starts at acquisition. You cannot outdeliver a bad creator selection forever
acquisition is a quality game or a volume game depending on the business model, depending on the deal structure

findings.topic: Chat performance tracking within delivery operating system
findings.title: Track chat performance weekly as a core delivery KPI — don't discover underperformance after revenue drops
findings.claim: Chatters underperforming is typically caught too late because there is no proactive weekly tracking system — by the time revenue drops, the damage is already done.
findings.what_they_do: Dylan embeds chat performance as one of the seven required pieces of a delivery operating system: content posted, PPV performance, rebuilds, expired fans, chat performance, creator compliance, creator complaints. The chatter manager owns chat revenue and quality as their primary number. Weekly scorecard review catches a declining chatter before the month closes. He contrasts reactive delivery (everyone panics after revenue drops) with proactive delivery (problem spotted before it becomes painful via data and tracking).
findings.vs_us: Palm currently outsources DM monetization to an external chat team (§8) and has no internal chat performance SOP. The whale-hunting module and Fan Tracker CRM (§9) capture some fan-side data, but there is no weekly chat performance scorecard tied to the delivery review cadence. This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team and AI chatting roadmap projects (§8).
findings.recommendation: As Palm moves toward in-house chatting, define the chat performance KPIs that the future chatter manager will own: PPV conversion rate, average revenue per fan per week, response time, welcome message performance, and reactivation rate on going-cold fans. Build these into the weekly scorecard from day one of in-house operations rather than retrofitting later. In the interim, request these metrics from the external chat team weekly and add them to Palm's existing Fan Tracker / whale-hunting reporting (§9) to start building baseline data now.

sources.quote: you spot the chatter that's performance is declining before the month closes out and the revenue drops
A chatter misses standards and nothing happens. That's the culture.
the chatter start to underperform. Someone notices too late cuz the chat manager slacked off

findings.topic: Instagram DM outreach volume and rate-limit management
findings.title: Send 50 IG DMs/day; back off 5 below rate-limit trigger
findings.claim: Sending ~50 DMs per day from a personal IG account is the optimal beginner outreach volume, and you should drop 5 below whatever number triggers Instagram's rate-limit warning to avoid restrictions.
findings.what_they_do: Operator DMs from their personal IG account targeting creators with 5k–50k followers who mention or have OnlyFans. They monitor for Instagram's 'Check community guidelines / actions required' warning; whatever number triggered it, they permanently send 5 fewer. Follow-up sequence: wait 24 hours after no reply, then send a voice message for personalization.
findings.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no documented daily volume target, rate-limit protocol, or follow-up cadence (§6 GAP). No internal baseline on outreach message structure or follow-up timing exists.
findings.recommendation: Codify a daily DM volume SOP: start at 50/day, instrument the rate-limit floor, and enforce a 24-hour → voice-message follow-up sequence. Document this in the acquisition runbook so it can be handed to a future acquisition VA.

sources.quote: I want you to send 50 DMs per day
always send five below. Whenever you get that message
wait 24 hours and then send another follow-up from that point on. a voice message

findings.topic: Outreach message framing — authority + personalization, no direct pitch
findings.title: Open with a conversion-problem hook; never pitch the agency in the first DM
findings.claim: The first DM should establish authority by identifying a specific problem in the creator's setup rather than pitching the agency, and should funnel toward a no-pressure discovery call rather than explaining the revenue split.
findings.what_they_do: Template opener: 'Hey [Name], I came across your socials across a few platforms and noticed a few things in your setup that might quietly be hurting conversions.' Then a brief authority statement. Never mention the split in DMs — save that for the call. On the call: ask current earnings, show what's possible, then close on next steps and send a contract via Dropbox.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented outreach message template or sales-call funnel (§6 GAP). The split is set per-creator at onboarding but no pre-call qualifying script exists (§7 GAP).
findings.recommendation: Draft a 2–3 message DM sequence template: (1) problem-hook opener with authority, (2) 24-hr follow-up voice note, (3) call-to-action to book a 10–15 min discovery call. Create a call script with qualifying questions (current earnings, goals) before revealing the split. Store both in the acquisition runbook.

sources.quote: Hey Name, I came across your socials across a few platforms and noticed a few things in your setup that might quietly be hurting conversions
Don't explain the split on the DMs. Obviously, explain the split on the actual call itself

findings.topic: Creator quality bar — A-tier targeting vs. settling
findings.title: Hold out for A-tier creators; 3 months with a weak creator costs more than 3 months outreaching
findings.claim: Spending months outreaching for a single high-quality creator yields more revenue and less wasted effort than onboarding an easy-to-sign but unmotivated creator.
findings.what_they_do: Griffin explicitly refuses to sign creators who don't film on time, lack motivation, or don't meet appearance standards. He targets creators with 5k–50k followers who already have or look suitable for OnlyFans. He argues a bad 3-month engagement versus a 3-month search for an A-tier creator is a false economy because agency leverage multiplies creator quality.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator qualification criteria or scoring system (§6 GAP). Onboarding form captures name, email, commission %, state — but no quality or potential assessment.
findings.recommendation: Define a written creator qualification rubric (follower range, content consistency, responsiveness, existing OF presence, appearance tier) to be applied before any sales call is booked. Add a qualification score field to the Creators Airtable table so onboarding decisions are data-driven, not intuition-based.

sources.quote: if you were to spend those same exact 3 months outreaching and then sign your A tier creator, you can make not only $10,000, $20,000 a month
the better your creator is, the more your work will show for it

findings.topic: Pre-signing ops stack — Notion, Discord, Telegram
findings.title: Stand up the full ops stack (Notion + Discord + Telegram) before signing creator #1
findings.claim: Agency infrastructure — dashboards, chatter management hub, and content distribution channels — must be fully configured before onboarding the first creator so signing can be fast and error-free.
findings.what_they_do: Griffin uses: Notion for the agency dashboard and KPI tracking; Discord as the chatter management server (chatters plugged into one repeatable process as they're hired); Telegram for content distribution. All three are set up in the foundation phase, before any outreach begins.
findings.vs_us: Palm uses Airtable (HQ + Content Ops), Telegram (routing to Amin for posting), and Publer for scheduling (§5). Palm does NOT use Notion or Discord; chatter management is outsourced externally rather than managed in a Discord hub (§8). The stack overlap on Telegram is real, but Discord-based chatter management is absent.
findings.recommendation: As Palm builds its in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap, §8), evaluate Discord as the coordination layer for chatters rather than building a bespoke system. It's the consistent standard across competitor agencies and would minimize onboarding friction when hiring chatters. For now, document the existing Airtable + Telegram + Publer stack as the official SOP so new hires have a clear baseline.

sources.quote: get your notion dashboard set up, your team management, your Discord hub for chatting, your Telegram for content distribution. Everything needs to be set up before even signing a creator
manage these chatters in Discord from day one. That way when you hire more and more, it's just plugging them into the same process

findings.topic: Social content strategy — filler vs. vertical content split
findings.title: Two-content-type IG framework: 'filler' (lifestyle) keeps algorithm happy; 'vertical' (viral-engineered) drives new traffic
findings.claim: Posting 2–3 Reels per day split between algorithm-maintenance lifestyle content and virality-engineered vertical content is the primary Instagram growth method for driving OF subscriptions.
findings.what_they_do: 2–3 Reels/day. Filler content = lifestyle/personal content to keep the account active and algorithm-fed. Vertical content = explicitly engineered for virality — Snapchat-style verticals, talking-head videos, mirror videos. Both types use 'link in bio' and pinned comments directing to OF. Competitor analysis is central: identify top-performing competitor videos, isolate the 3 best, test posting similar styles, then use performance data to teach the creator what to film and why it works (lighting, facial expression, movement).
findings.vs_us: Palm has a mature 8-step inspo-driven pipeline that uses viral data to decide what creators film (§1), which directly parallels the competitor analysis piece here. However, Palm's posting cadence (frequency, time-of-day) is not codified (§1 GAP). The filler vs. vertical content taxonomy is not explicitly documented in Palm's system.
findings.recommendation: Formally adopt the filler/vertical taxonomy inside Palm's content pipeline. Tag each Asset in Airtable as 'filler' or 'vertical/traffic-driver' so editors and schedulers can enforce the 2–3/day cadence with the right mix. This codifies the cadence GAP noted in §1.

sources.quote: Filler content is just going to be her lifestyle. This keeps the algorithm happy… Vertical content is what brings new people into your page
Find three competitors. Make a list of the top performing videos. Take the three most performing videos. Put that in a style selection process

findings.topic: Chatting ratio KPI and chatter hire threshold
findings.title: Target 1:10 sub-to-chat revenue ratio; hire chatters only after reaching $2–5K/month agency revenue
findings.claim: A 1:10 ratio of subscription revenue to DM/tip revenue is the core chatting KPI, and chatters should not be hired until the agency earns $2–5K/month to ensure they're affordable.
findings.what_they_do: Chatting ratio defined as: for every $1 in subscription revenue, target $10 in DM+tip revenue. Benchmarks: 1:6 = decent, 1:8 = good, 1:10 = great, 1:15 = excellent. Hire VA chatters at $3–$5/hour + commission once agency hits $2–5K/month. Manage chatters via Discord. Welcome message framework: thank sub, ask their name, build rapport → send teasers → sell PPVs → upsell. Chatting is treated as a strategy, not ad-hoc.
findings.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) with no internal KPI, ratio target, or hire-threshold documented. This is directly relevant to Palm's ~1yr roadmap to bring chatting in-house and the active AI chatting project. The 1:10 ratio is a concrete benchmark Palm should adopt as a performance target for the external team today and the in-house team when built.
findings.recommendation: Immediately: share the 1:10 sub-to-chat ratio benchmark with the external chat team as a performance SLA and track it in the existing Fan Tracker / whale-hunting infrastructure (§9). Build-toward: use the $3–$5/hour + commission pay structure and Discord management model as the template for in-house chatter hiring. Add 'chatting ratio' as a tracked metric in the Airtable reporting layer.

sources.quote: for every $1 you have in the chats, you want to have $10 in the or in the subs. You want to have $10 in the chats. So we stick to 1 to 10
once you're paying or making 2 to 5K a month, hire a VA to chat. So, if you're making, you know, 1K a month, 700 bucks a month, do not hire chatters

findings.topic: Onboarding sequence — OF page setup before social access
findings.title: Structured 7-day onboarding: OF page + vault first (days 1–4), social access + content plan second (days 5–7)
findings.claim: A sequenced onboarding where the OF page is optimized and chatting infrastructure is ready before social accounts are touched reduces errors and accelerates time-to-revenue.
findings.what_they_do: Days 1–2: get OF login and Inflow access, optimize vault and OF page. Days 3–4: bio, subscription price, page optimization. Days 5–7: gain IG/TikTok/Twitter access, set up content distribution, scripts, chatting setup, and build content plan with creator. Content plan uses the 3-competitor research process (top videos → style selection → post → teach creator what to film and why).
findings.vs_us: Palm's onboarding form captures credentials and contract (§6) but there is no documented day-by-day onboarding sequence or ordering logic. The OF vault/PPV system is thin (§2) and no OF page optimization checklist exists. The social setup is mature for AI accounts (§4) but not formalized for real creators.
findings.recommendation: Build a real-creator onboarding SOP mirroring the AI warmup cadence structure (§4): Days 1–4 = OF access + page/vault optimization checklist; Days 5–7 = social access + content plan creation. Add this as a checklist workflow in Airtable under the Creators table, triggered on creator status change to 'onboarding.'

sources.quote: Log in to the only fans in the inflow account. Optimize the vault, optimize the OF page. Then on days like seven, I want you to get into the Instagram, Tik Tok, and Twitter accounts
content strategy and create a content plan with her. So on that, I want you to do niche competitor research

findings.topic: Agency pricing sweet spot
findings.title: Charge $1,500–$2,500/month per client to hit $10K–$20K with 4–8 clients
findings.claim: The sweet spot for agency pricing is $1,500–$2,500/month, allowing a solo operator to hit $10K/month with just four clients rather than needing 50+ low-ticket clients.
findings.what_they_do: Targets $1,500–$2,500/month retainers per client. At $2,500 x 4 clients = $10K/month; 8 clients = $20K/month. One person can manage up to 25–26 clients at this tier before needing to hire.
findings.vs_us: Palm's commission structure is % of creator revenue (§7), not a flat retainer — so pricing model differs structurally. However, Palm has no documented rationale for its split percentages or floor minimums (§7 🔲 GAP). The 'fewer high-value clients beats many low-value clients' logic maps directly to Palm's creator portfolio sizing question.
findings.recommendation: Use the $1,500–$2,500 benchmark as a reference when modeling minimum viable creator revenue per account Palm should onboard. Define a floor: if a creator is unlikely to generate enough revenue to justify Palm's management overhead, don't sign them. Document the commission split rationale explicitly to fill the §7 gap.

sources.quote: 1,500 to 2,500, which is a nice sweet spot, you can. Because let's do the math here. 4 * 2,500 10K.

findings.topic: Productized service delivery
findings.title: Productize one repeatable service per niche so delivery is copy-paste, not custom each time
findings.claim: Offering a single systematized, repeatable service instead of custom work for every client is what makes an agency scalable and sustainable.
findings.what_they_do: Picks one niche and one core service (e.g., Google Ads lead gen to a landing page). Builds the campaign once, then copies and pastes the structure for every new client. Learns from aggregate campaign data across all clients to improve results over time. Explicitly avoids mixing service types (websites for one client, Google Ads for another, Facebook for a third).
findings.vs_us: Palm's social content pipeline (§1) is already productized — inspo scrape → analyze → film → edit → post runs the same way for every creator. The OF vault/PPV side (§2) is thin and non-productized. This finding reinforces Palm's existing strength on social and flags that PPV content production needs a similar systematized playbook.
findings.recommendation: Apply the same copy-paste productization logic to OF vault/PPV content (§2 gap): define a standard PPV content set structure (e.g., X pieces per week, price tiers, release cadence) that can be templated across all creators rather than decided ad hoc per creator.

sources.quote: once we've decided on the niche, we want to offer productized services, which means a service that we can offer again and again in a very systematized, very automated way

findings.topic: Target client qualification by company size
findings.title: Qualify prospects by employee count as a proxy for $1M–$10M revenue range
findings.claim: Businesses with 2–30 employees are typically in the $1M–$10M revenue range — large enough to afford agency services, small enough that you can reach the decision-maker directly.
findings.what_they_do: Uses employee headcount as a quick qualifier: ~2–3 employees signals ~$1M revenue; ~20–30 employees signals ~$10M. This range means the prospect has marketing budget AND the owner/decision-maker is still reachable without going through corporate gatekeepers.
findings.vs_us: Palm's creator acquisition is entirely Josh-DM-led with no documented qualification criteria (§6 🔲 GAP). There is no equivalent 'signal' Palm uses to decide which creators to pursue. The underlying logic — identify a proxy signal that predicts both willingness to pay AND accessibility — is directly applicable.
findings.recommendation: Define a Palm creator qualification scorecard analogous to employee-count proxy: e.g., existing follower count range, current OF revenue floor, niche fit, posting frequency. Creators too small won't generate enough revenue; creators too large may already have management. Document this to systematize Josh's outbound decisions (§6).

sources.quote: at the 1 million mark, we'll say two to three employees. And then you can just do the math and work that up to 20 to 30 on the high end

findings.topic: Low-ticket volume vs high-ticket concentration
findings.title: Avoid low-ticket volume models: getting 2 premium clients is orders of magnitude easier than getting 50 low-ticket ones
findings.claim: Acquiring two high-paying clients requires far less effort than acquiring fifty low-paying ones, making low-ticket volume models a trap for agency owners.
findings.what_they_do: Frames the math explicitly: 50 clients at $97/month = $5,000; 4 clients at $2,500 = $10,000 with a fraction of the acquisition and support overhead. Argues that there is 'not really that much extra work' to target higher-paying clients but the upside is dramatically better.
findings.vs_us: Palm's revenue is creator-revenue-share-based, so the direct pricing analogy doesn't apply, but the portfolio concentration principle does. If Palm signs many small creators with low earning potential to hit volume, management overhead scales while revenue per creator stays low. No documented policy on portfolio size vs. creator quality exists (§6, §7 🔲 GAP).
findings.recommendation: Establish a minimum viable creator revenue threshold before signing. Prioritize signing 4–8 high-earning creators over 20+ micro-earners. Document this as a founding portfolio strategy principle to guide Josh's acquisition decisions and Evan's ops planning.

sources.quote: getting two clients, even at a higher price point, is orders of magnitude easier than getting 20, 30, 40, 50 lower paying clients

findings.topic: Personalized client communication as differentiation
findings.title: Differentiate from automated competitors by calling clients and treating them as real people
findings.claim: At premium price points, agencies can and should offer direct human communication — calls, responsive emails, real answers — which becomes the actual differentiator against lower-priced automated competitors.
findings.what_they_do: Explicitly offers phone calls, responsive emails, and real conversations with clients as part of service delivery. Frames this as only economically viable at $1,500–$2,500+/month (can't afford it at $97). Positions human accessibility as the competitive moat against SaaS/AI commodity offerings.
findings.vs_us: Palm's creator relationships are managed by Josh (acquisition/strategy) but there is no documented creator success cadence, check-in frequency, or retention SOP (§9 🔲 GAP). Creators are the 'clients' in Palm's model. The risk of churn from creators feeling unsupported is real and undocumented.
findings.recommendation: Define a creator success cadence: e.g., weekly performance update (automated from portal data), bi-weekly check-in call with Josh or Evan, monthly revenue review. Document this as Palm's retention differentiator. This directly addresses the §9 gap and reduces creator churn risk.

sources.quote: we're going to be normal human beings. That's right. We're going to call them. We're going to send them emails. We're going to answer their questions.

findings.topic: Solo operator scaling ceiling
findings.title: One person can manage ~25 clients before needing to hire; use contractors/white-label before full-time staff
findings.claim: A solo agency operator can sustainably manage 25–26 clients at premium pricing before the workload justifies building a contractor or outsourcer team.
findings.what_they_do: Ran 25–26 clients solo (described as 'probably excessive'). At that point, brought in freelancers, outsourcers, and contractors — not full-time employees. Points to white-label fulfillment as a scaling mechanism that avoids direct hiring overhead.
findings.vs_us: Palm currently has Josh, Evan, Amin (contractor), editors, and an external chat team (§10). The team structure is thin and not formally documented. There is no scaling plan or org chart (§10 🔲 GAP). The contractor-before-FTE model Palm already implicitly uses (Amin, external chat) aligns with this advice.
findings.recommendation: Document Palm's explicit scaling model: at what creator/revenue count does each next hire (editor, VA, account manager, chatter) get triggered? Use the '25 clients solo' benchmark as a reference point. Formalize the contractor-first hiring ladder before Palm's creator roster grows to a point where ad hoc decisions create ops gaps.

sources.quote: the most clients that I ever ran with just myself was like 25, 26 clients. It was probably excessive, but the money was there, so it justified it. And then at that point, I started looking for help
